ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for an energy bowl beef brisket soup meal. FOODON_00002764 (beef soup) is the closest defensible parent. The bowl-meal composite format is not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Beef meat dishes) is the closest parent category. No specific code exists for a braised beef brisket soup bowl meal in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Beef soup) is the closest available concept. The composite energy bowl meal format is not individually represented in SNOMED CT.

allergens: gluten (if noodles or soy sauce used)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 500 g, comprising a bowl of braised beef brisket soup with a carbohydrate base (rice or noodles) and accompanying vegetables, as typically served at a Singapore food outlet.

nutrient_source.title: Beef brisket soup with rice — composite dish estimation based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Beef, brisket, flat half, separable lean and fat, braised (NDB 13326) and Soup, beef broth or bouillon, ready-to-serve (NDB 06159), combined with cooked white rice (NDB 20444)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Energy Bowl Beef Brisket Soup Meal' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition: approximately 35% braised beef brisket (lean and fat), 30% beef broth, 25% cooked white rice, and 10% mixed vegetables. Per-100g values are derived from this composite. USDA SR Legacy entries used: NDB 13326 (beef brisket braised), NDB 06159 (beef broth ready-to-serve), NDB 20444 (white rice cooked), NDB 11583 (mixed vegetables). Sodium is elevated due to broth component. Values represent a plausible central estimate for this dish category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) GI ~64; beef broth and braised beef contribute negligible glycemic load; composite bowl GI estimated at 54 based on carbohydrate fraction from rice component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite bowl meal. GI is estimated using the carbohydrate-weighted contribution method. The primary glycemic carbohydrate source is cooked white rice (GI ~64 per University of Sydney GI Database). Beef brisket and broth contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Mixed vegetables contribute minimal glycemic load. The composite GI is estimated at 54 (low), reflecting dilution of the rice GI by the protein, fat, and broth components which slow gastric emptying. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (54 × 55 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 29.7. Carbohydrate per serving estimated at 55 g (11.0 g per 100 g × 500 g serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from beef broth
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (estimated 1600 mg per 500 g serving), this meal provides a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium broth or consume a smaller portion of the soup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice carbohydrate base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 29.7 per standard serving and a low GI of 54, this meal can cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller rice portion, substituting with a lower-GI grain, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef brisket and elevated sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef brisket contributes saturated fat (approximately 1.6 g per 100 g) and the broth contributes high sodium, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD should limit portion size, choose leaner cuts where available, and avoid drinking the full volume of broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Energy Bowl Beef Brisket Soup Meal is a hearty, restaurant-style bowl dish served in Singapore, combining slow-braised or long-simmered beef brisket in a rich, savoury beef broth with a carbohydrate base — typically steamed white rice or noodles — and a portion of mixed vegetables. Beef brisket is a tough, collagen-rich cut from the lower chest of the animal that becomes tender and flavourful after extended braising or simmering, yielding a deeply umami broth. The dish is warming, filling, and satisfying, with a soft, melt-in-the-mouth texture from the slow-cooked beef contrasting with the firmness of the rice or noodles. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density of approximately 112 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 8.5 g per 100 g from the beef, moderate fat at 4.2 g per 100 g, and carbohydrate at 11.0 g per 100 g primarily from the rice or noodle base. The dish is notable for its high sodium content (approximately 320 mg per 100 g) due to the broth, which is a clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low), driven by the rice component, making portion control of the carbohydrate base important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is commonly found at Singapore food courts, hawker-style outlets, and casual dining restaurants, and is positioned as a complete, energy-sustaining meal. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and may contain gluten if soy sauce or wheat-based noodles are used.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Energy Bowl Beef Brisket Soup Meal' was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for braised beef brisket, beef broth, cooked white rice, and mixed vegetables. GI is an analogous estimate based on the rice carbohydrate fraction using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the component-level sources are approved and defensible, but the composite proportions are assumed rather than measured.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on USDA SR Legacy data for individual components (braised beef brisket NDB 13326, beef broth NDB 06159, cooked white rice NDB 20444, mixed vegetables NDB 11583). No direct laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry was found for this specific dish. 2. Sodium is elevated due to the broth component; actual sodium may vary significantly depending on the restaurant's recipe and broth concentration. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimation. 4. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimation. 5. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data. 6. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 7. The cuisine tag 'Sandwich' in the input data appears to be a field survey platform categorisation artefact and does not reflect the actual cuisine type of this dish; cuisine has been corrected to 'Western-Asian Fusion'. 8. Halal status is not confirmed; beef brisket dishes in Singapore may or may not be halal-certified depending on the restaurant. 9. The serving size of 500 g is an estimate for a standard bowl meal; actual serving size may vary by restaurant.
